attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old woman is brought to see her general practitioner by her son, who is concerned about his mother's tremors. These have been gradually worsening over the last six months and are most noticeable when she extends her arms. The tremors are jerky and irregular. The son has also noticed a decline in his mother's balance and changes in her speech.

Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension, for which she takes metformin and perindopril. She drinks a few glasses of wine most nights and occasionally more on weekends. She was recently on a short course of metoclopramide for vomiting associated with a bout of gastroenteritis. Her sister was recently diagnosed with Parkinson's disease and had to relocate to a nursing home due to dementia.

On examination, the patient displays an unsteady, broad-based gait, breaks words into syllables while speaking ("chocolate brownie" pronounced as "cho-co-late brow-nie"), and exhibits difficulty in quickly flipping one hand over the other.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient is most likely experiencing a cerebellar tremor. Signs consistent with cerebellar lesions can be summarised with the mnemonic PINARDS: Past pointing, Intention tremor, Nystagmus, Ataxia, Rebound, Dysdiadokinesia, Slurred speech. In this case, the woman's tremors are described as jerky and manifest with arm extension, matching the characteristics of intention tremor. Additionally, the presence of an unsteady, broad-based gait and changes in speech is consistent with ataxia and slurred speech. Cerebellar tremor can result from multiple sclerosis, trauma, or stroke, or any lesion affecting cerebellar pathways, including connections to the brainstem and thalamus.

Essential tremor is one of the most common movement disorders worldwide and is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. Similar to cerebellar tremor, it most commonly occurs during action but is peculiarly improved by alcohol. It usually affects both upper limbs. The recent onset of the tremor with associated ataxia and slurred speech puts essential tremor lower on the differential list.

Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterised by the presence of 2 of the 3 classic motor features (rest tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity) and a sustained response to dopaminergic therapy. Although the patient does have a positive family history, her tremor is very unlikely to be related to PD, given that it is an intention tremor.

Metoclopramide is an antiemetic that works by blocking the central dopamine receptor. Given that its mechanism of action exerts a similar effect as dopamine depletion in PD, one would expect it to cause a resting tremor rather than an intention tremor. It is also unlikely for a recent course of metoclopramide to have been the major cause of a tremor that started six months ago.

Flapping tremor (asterixis) is the sudden loss of muscle tone during the sustained contraction of an outstretched limb. It can be caused by liver failure (hepatic flap), renal failure/azotemia, and CO2 narcosis (type 2 respiratory failure). Historically, her alcohol intake is unlikely to have caused hepatic encephalopathy but remains an important consideration. However, asterixis is unlikely, given that the tremor does not occur only on dorsiflexion, and the patient has no current signs of chronic liver disease or hepatic encephalopathy. Of note, alcohol-induced cerebellar ataxia can occur independently of the metabolic encephalopathy associated with chronic liver disease. Gaze‐evoked nystagmus induced by alcohol intoxication often presents with a more vertical nystagmus rather than a horizontal one.
learningPoints: Signs consistent with cerebellar lesions can be summarised with the mnemonic PINARDS: Past pointing, Intention tremor, Nystagmus, Ataxia, Rebound, Dysdiadokinesia, Slurred speech.
Action tremor can be caused by essential tremor and cerebellar tremor (commonly described as intention tremor).
Rest tremor are most commonly caused by Parkinson's disease or other parkinsonian plus syndromes.
references: Sirisena D, Williams DR. My hands shake: Classification and treatment of tremor. Australian family physician. 2009 Sep;38(9):678-83. https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/4b03a304-f3a5-4571-917f-ee52f594808e/Classification-and-tr... Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19893794/
Bhidayasiri R, Tarsy D, Bhidayasiri R, Tarsy D. Cerebellar Tremor. Movement Disorders: A Video Atlas: A Video Atlas. 2012:54-5. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-60327-426-5_26

Romano F, Tarnutzer AA, Straumann D, Ramat S, Bertolini G. Gaze-evoked nystagmus induced by alcohol intoxication. J Physiol. 2017 Mar 15;595(6):2161-2173. doi: 10.1113/JP273204. Epub 2017 Jan 17. PMID: 27981586; PMCID: PMC5350443. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27981586/
